video_title: Privacy Alts Are Pumping While Bitcoin & ETH Are Weakening
gist: Bankless weekly roundup (last week of May 2026): BTC fell below $73K with the second-largest IBIT outflow since debut while ETH broke below $2,000, yet a narrow set of 'consensus' alts (Hyperliquid/HYPE, Near, Venice/VVV, Zcash, Railgun) hit all-time highs. Hosts debate whether this privacy/up-and-comer rotation can survive a major breakdown (Berniske/Berninski says no), David Hoffman explains why he sold all his ETH, and a privacy-as-the-surviving-use-case thesis runs through Vitalik's EF refocus on ETH-the-asset and Near's confidential-intents adoption.

insights.claim: Bitcoin fell ~5% on the week to roughly $73,000 (from $78,000), retracing back toward recent ranging lows and erasing the gains it made during the Iran war.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: BTC giving back its geopolitical-hedge gains signals the safe-haven bid has faded; watch the recent ranging lows as the line that decides whether 'cycles are just cycling.'
insights.quote: Bitcoin's coming in right at $73,000. It is uh down down from $78,000 on the week.

insights.claim: BlackRock's IBIT saw its second-largest single-day outflow since debut, including a single ~$1.3 billion IBIT sell, and the last 12 days ranked in the bottom 5% of worst inflow/outflow days in Bitcoin ETF history.
insights.speaker: Ryan Sean Adams
insights.so_what: Sustained record-bad ETF flows mean the marginal institutional buyer has turned seller; ETF flow data is the cleanest real-time tape for BTC demand.
insights.quote: the last 12 days have been the bottom 5% of uh worst performing days for inflows outflows

insights.claim: Michael Nado interprets the large IBIT selling as front-running macro worries, casting Bitcoin again as a 'canary in the coal mine' for macro trouble ahead.
insights.speaker: Ryan Sean Adams (citing Michael Nado)
insights.so_what: If BTC is leading macro stress, weakness here may presage broader risk-off; treat crypto outflows as an early macro indicator rather than a crypto-specific story.
insights.quote: Michael's take is this is like front running some macro worries right just bitcoin again being kind of canary in the coal mine

insights.claim: ETH fell ~6.2% on the week and broke below $2,000 to ~$1,995, with hosts framing this as the worst Ethereum sentiment since 2019.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: ETH underperforming BTC into a sub-$2K capitulation with 2019-level sentiment is a classic contrarian setup to watch, but only if a major-driven washout completes.
insights.quote: ether down 6.2% 2% below $2,000. We're at $1995.

insights.claim: ETH staking has risen to ~31% of supply and the staking queue is up, which David argues is not a clean 'who's left to sell' bottom signal because staked ETH can still be unstaked and sold.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman / Ryan Sean Adams
insights.so_what: Don't treat a rising staking ratio as locked-up float; supply can re-enter the market, so it's a weak floor for ETH price.
insights.quote: look at the staking queue, you know, it's up. There's, you know, 31% ETH staking. Who's left to sell?

themes.theme: Rotation into privacy and consensus alts

insights.claim: A narrow 'four horsemen' set of alts (Hyperliquid/HYPE, Near, Venice/VVV, Zcash) plus Railgun hit or neared all-time highs in late May 2026 even as BTC and ETH broke down, an unusual divergence for a bear market.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: Money is concentrating into a privacy/up-and-comer basket; these are the momentum names to track, but their fate is tethered to whether the majors hold.
insights.quote: It's uh Hyperliquid, Near uh Venice, and uh Zcash all hitting basically all-time highs. That doesn't usually happen during a bare market.

insights.claim: After topping out, these leaders have already pulled back sharply: HYPE from $64.40 ATH to ~$57, VVV from just over $20 to $14.88, Near from $2.88 to $2.33, and Zcash from $682 to ~$535.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: Double-digit drawdowns from ATHs as BTC slid ~80k to ~72k show the alt strength is fragile and high-beta to Bitcoin; size positions for sharp reversals.
insights.quote: Hype is now down from 64 to 57. VVV hit an all-time high of just over $20. It's now at 14.88. Near hit $2.88. It's now at $2.33. And then Zcash hit an all-time high of $682. It's currently coming in at 535.

insights.claim: David argues the alt strength is structurally thin: these 'consensus coins' are bid by perhaps only ~50 remaining crypto-Twitter participants, so the rally lacks broad demand and is not sustainable if Bitcoin drops.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: Treat the alt ATHs as low-liquidity, low-float moves vulnerable to a vacuum bid; thin consensus rallies reverse violently when the marginal buyer steps away.
insights.quote: there are so few people left in crypto that it doesn't matter if these tokens are consensus cuz there's just like 50 people buying these things that are left and that's all of crypto Twitter.

insights.claim: Chris Berniske's bear thesis: there is no durable paradigm where majors are rotated out of and up-and-comers sustain a swim against an outgoing tide; the majors are 'telling us something' and the alt pump will not last if BTC/ETH keep falling.
insights.speaker: Chris Berniske (cited by David Hoffman)
insights.so_what: The dominant analytical view is that alt outperformance is a head-fake; a BTC breakdown of even ~$10K would drag the whole complex, so the alt rotation is a fade for risk managers.
insights.quote: I don't see a new paradigm where majors are rotated out of and select up and upandcomers can sustain their swim against an outgoing tide for long.

insights.claim: Mike Nado's framework (developed with David): the market is at a make-or-break level where BTC rising from here would be unprecedented bear-market strength, but a drop confirms 'the cycles are simply cycling.'
insights.speaker: David Hoffman (citing Michael Nado)
insights.so_what: Defines a binary technical decision point; a confirmed breakdown validates a normal four-year-cycle top and argues for de-risking the whole complex.
insights.quote: if Bitcoin goes up for here from here it's pretty unprecedented in terms of like bare market strength but if it goes down from here then the cycles are simply cycling.

insights.claim: Hyperliquid ETFs (chiefly Bitwise BHY and 21Shares TYP) launched ~1-2 weeks ago and have drawn over $101 million of inflows, with Grayscale (GHY) and reportedly VanEck also filing.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: TradFi is gaining wrapped HYPE exposure via brokerages; strong early inflows make Hyperliquid the standout institutional alt-adoption story to track.
insights.quote: There's over $101 million of inflows since their launch earlier this month. This is mostly between the Bitwise BHY and the 21 Shares TYP uh ETFs.

insights.claim: A single Hyperliquid trader is profiting by going long S&P 500 and NASDAQ while shorting ~12-13 cryptos (ETH, MEGA, Fartcoin, TRUMP, Athena, LayerZero, zkSync, Bitcoin Cash, UNI, Cardano, Litecoin, XRP), with every long green and every short green simultaneously.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: The dominant working trade is long equities / short crypto beta; the regime favors equity AI exposure over crypto, and crypto-shorts as a basket are paying.
insights.quote: just go long equities and short crypto and you can make money

insights.claim: ETH is the single most-targeted crypto short, with the example trader running a $10 million, 25x short on ETH (his largest position) rather than shorting Bitcoin, reflecting a market belief ETH can be shorted 'more safely' than BTC.
insights.speaker: Ryan Sean Adams / David Hoffman
insights.so_what: Concentrated, leveraged ETH shorts are a positioning signal that ETH is the consensus underperformer, but also sets up squeeze risk if sentiment turns.
insights.quote: This is a $10 million position on ETH. It's a 25x uh position on ETH

insights.claim: No US-regulated perpetuals license exists yet, so the onshore US perps market is an open 'golden goose'; Polymarket is now building a perps platform (beta live for select users) and Coinbase/Robinhood are positioning, with no clear winner.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman / Ryan Sean Adams
insights.so_what: Whoever first wins a compliant US perps license captures the largest capital pool; this is a major land-grab to watch among Polymarket, Coinbase, Robinhood and Hyperliquid.
insights.quote: it's really the regulated compliant onshore market that is really the golden goose. I mean United States is still the center of capital.

insights.claim: The community-supported Hyperliquid DAT 'PURR' (Hyperliquid Strategies) is the only one of roughly 30 remaining digital-asset-treasury vehicles trading in the green.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: The DAT model is broadly underwater except where the underlying (HYPE) is outperforming; underlying-asset strength, not the treasury wrapper, drives DAT returns.
insights.quote: it is the only DAT out of all of them like the 30 of them that I think remain that is in the green.

insights.claim: MicroStrategy/Strategy's average BTC cost basis is ~$75,700; it has spent ~$63 billion to acquire BTC now worth ~$61 billion, leaving the position ~$2 billion underwater.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: Saylor's cost basis sitting at ~$75.7K (above spot ~$73K) marks a key level; an underwater Strategy is unusual and worth watching for forced-behavior or sentiment effects.
insights.quote: the average Bitcoin cost basis for Strategy is $75,700 uh dollar bitcoin. Strategy has spent a total of $63 billion to acquire what is now worth $61 billion of Bitcoin.

insights.claim: Strategy used dry powder to repurchase/clear convertible notes rather than buy Bitcoin this week, which the hosts read as a sign that Strategy's Bitcoin buying is tapering.
insights.speaker: Ryan Sean Adams / David Hoffman
insights.so_what: If the largest structural BTC buyer is paying down liabilities instead of accumulating, a key demand pillar is weakening; watch Strategy's purchase cadence.
insights.quote: using some of their dry powder to go short up their balance sheet rather than buy Bitcoin, which gives you the sense that the Bitcoin buys are tapering.

insights.claim: Tom Lee's BitMine is down ~$8 billion on an ~$18 billion Ether position, a far deeper drawdown than Strategy's, framing it as either a generational entry or a badly-timed trade given it started under a year ago.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman / Ryan Sean Adams
insights.so_what: An ETH treasury down ~44% concentrates ETH downside risk in one large, recent holder; BitMine's solvency/conviction is a key ETH overhang to monitor.
insights.quote: Bit mine down $8 billion on their position on their much smaller position uh of Ether... $8 billion being down $8 billion on $18 billion is a very different story

insights.claim: Mark Cuban has sold most of his Bitcoin, saying it 'lost the plot' and failed as a hedge: during the Iran war gold ran to ~$5,000 while Bitcoin dropped, and BTC didn't rise when the dollar fell as a fiat-debasement hedge should.
insights.speaker: Mark Cuban (clip)
insights.so_what: A prominent ex-bull abandoning the digital-gold thesis on the basis that BTC failed its first real geopolitical/debasement test is a sentiment marker and a challenge to the macro-hedge narrative.
insights.quote: gold just blew up and went, you know, to $5,000, Bitcoin dropped. And every time the dollar dropped, Bitcoin should have gone up... and it just didn't do that.

insights.claim: Cuban says crypto is disappointing because it never built 'an application for grandma' (a mainstream consumer killer app analogous to the iPhone app store), and dismisses memecoins/token stuff as 'garbage,' while being less disappointed in Ethereum than Bitcoin.
insights.speaker: Mark Cuban (clip)
insights.so_what: The consumer-adoption thesis remains unfulfilled per a sophisticated former bull; absent a mainstream app, the asset case rests on store-of-value/DeFi, not mass utility.
insights.quote: crypto is disappointed because it hasn't come up with an application for grandma.

insights.claim: David Hoffman's counter-framework: ascribing a fixed model to Bitcoin (e.g., 'it must rally in an Iran war') guarantees disappointment because we don't actually understand how or why Bitcoin trades.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: Cautions against trading BTC as a deterministic macro hedge; its behavior is regime-dependent and not reliably predictable from a single narrative.
insights.quote: I I think if you over overcribe a model onto Bitcoin, you're you're destined to be disappointed.

insights.claim: Polymarket odds for 2026 are deeply bearish: ~55% chance BTC hits $55K and ~42% chance $50K, with only ~50% odds of $90K (vs $140K ATH) and ~20% odds of $35K; ETH shows ~60% odds of $1,500, ~21% of $3,500 and only ~5% of $6,500.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman / Ryan Sean Adams
insights.so_what: Prediction markets are pricing meaningful downside (1-in-5 chance of $35K BTC) and weak upside; a useful sentiment/positioning gauge that confirms bearish skew.
insights.quote: we got a 55% chance that Bitcoin will hit $55,000... There's almost a 20% chance that we get to 35k Bitcoin at some point this year.

insights.claim: Mark Cuban also quit Bitcoin saying it is 'worse than gold' this week, reinforcing the digital-gold-vs-gold underperformance narrative as a recurring bear theme.
insights.speaker: Ryan Sean Adams
insights.so_what: Reinforces that BTC has lost the gold-substitute argument in this regime; relative performance vs gold is the key narrative battleground for BTC's macro case.
insights.quote: Mark Cuban also quit Bitcoin this week. Uh says it's worse than gold.

themes.theme: David Hoffman's 'why I sold my ETH' thesis

insights.claim: David Hoffman sold all of his ETH after being pro-ETH since 2017-2018, arguing the 'strong crypto' environment (cypherpunk alternative financial system, DeFi/NFT/DAO peak of 2021) that made ETH valuable as money hasn't existed since 2021, while 'weak crypto' (a back-end efficiency upgrade for big financial institutions) is now the meta.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: A founding ETH bull capitulating on the monetary thesis is a major sentiment event; the strong-vs-weak-crypto frame argues ETH's monetary premium depends on a macro/cultural regime that has gone.
insights.quote: strong crypto has never felt weaker to me now in 2026 because weak crypto, which is just like a back-end efficiency upgrade for the world's biggest financial institutions, seems to be the meta.

insights.claim: David's core L1 valuation framework: no layer-1 will ever have sustainable fees because chains perpetually expand block space and minimize fees, so L1s are 'all money or they're nothing' and ETH must win a head-to-head competition to be the best money.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: Reframes smart-contract L1 valuation away from revenue/fees toward monetary premium; implies ETH is undervalued only if it wins the money competition, otherwise it's worth little.
insights.quote: no layer 1 blockchain will actually have sustainable fees into the future. They're all money or they're nothing.

insights.claim: David argues ETH-as-money required Ethereum to maintain ~95% smart-contract dominance to be valued as money; because Solana 'nipped at its heels' and took users/revenue, dominance fell toward ~65%, so the market now values ETH as a (revenue-dependent) smart-contract chain instead.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: Ties ETH's lost monetary premium directly to lost market share; Ethereum's dominance percentage is the key variable determining whether it re-rates as money or as a cash-flow asset.
insights.quote: If it's 95% dominant, then it's likely that ETH is valued as money. If it's 65% dominant, then it's going to be valued as a smart contract chain.

insights.claim: David frames ETH as having 'skipped a cycle' (barely hitting ATHs last cycle, no overperformance), blaming an 'original sin': the rollup-centric roadmap and a culture that emphasized the Ethereum blockchain over ETH-the-asset and DeFi.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: Argues ETH's underperformance is self-inflicted via misallocated focus; a culture/roadmap pivot back to ETH-the-asset and DeFi would be the catalyst for any re-rating.
insights.quote: ETH effectively is skipping a cycle... Part of this had to do with a rollupcentric road map that felt like kind of a side quest in retrospect.

insights.claim: David's near-term ETH view is explicitly not bearish but neutral: he expects ETH to merely track the market with no path to a re-rating in either direction, comparing it to Microsoft ('too big to grow, too existential to die') or silver vs Bitcoin's gold.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: Even the bear case is 'beta with no catalyst,' implying ETH is a market-tracking hold rather than alpha; investors seeking outperformance should look elsewhere unless a re-rating catalyst appears.
insights.quote: I am and ether is about tracking the market is like kind of my base case. I don't see how it gets rerated in any direction.

insights.claim: Ryan Sean Adams's rebuttal: ETH still scores best on the actual properties of money (censorship resistance, decentralization, hardness of inflation schedule) with nothing close, and the monetary competition plays out over decades, so David's call is 'too early' rather than wrong.
insights.speaker: Ryan Sean Adams
insights.so_what: The bull counter is that ETH's monetary fundamentals are intact and only the timeframe is in question; a decades-long currency battle argues for patience, not exit, for long-horizon holders.
insights.quote: if you're looking for the properties of money, right, which is decentralized money... Ether and Ethereum score really well, and nothing else is really close.

insights.claim: Ryan argues Ethereum's residual bull case rests on ETH-as-money fundamentals still intact (DeFi, scaling, coming quantum resistance, and privacy via app-layer Railgun), making private multisig store-of-value a use case unmatched by Bitcoin or Zcash.
insights.speaker: Ryan Sean Adams
insights.so_what: Identifies private, programmable store-of-value as ETH's differentiated edge; if privacy ships at scale, it could be the catalyst that David says is currently missing.
insights.quote: if you're looking to store your value...multisig with privacy like that's not a bad route to go. I mean show me that on Bitcoin, show me that on Zcash.

themes.theme: Vitalik / EF refocus on ETH-the-asset and privacy

insights.claim: Vitalik says the Ethereum Foundation is shrinking and running low on ETH, so it is resizing to focus narrowly on 'crops'—censorship resistance, open source, privacy, and security—while pushing the rest of the ecosystem to handle everything else.
insights.speaker: Ryan Sean Adams (citing Vitalik)
insights.so_what: EF is concentrating on privacy and credible neutrality as the core deliverables; these become the roadmap items most likely to drive ETH's differentiated value.
insights.quote: Our focus is going to be crops. And so we're sizing the team and the foundation to support uh censorship resistance, open source, privacy, and security.

insights.claim: Vitalik stated unusually directly that the most financially high-value product of Ethereum is ETH-the-asset (securing ~$250B), that the protocol's properties are 'very good for ETH the asset,' and that ~90% of his net worth is in ETH with the remaining ~$40M of on-chain fiat fully allocated.
insights.speaker: Ryan Sean Adams (citing Vitalik)
insights.so_what: A long-awaited explicit endorsement of ETH-the-asset from Vitalik could begin to repair the 'original sin' of neglecting the asset; a narrative/culture pivot bullish for ETH framing.
insights.quote: the most high value product of the Ethereum blockchain financially speaking is ETH the asset Ethereum secures 250 billion of ETH

insights.claim: Standard Chartered likens ETH-the-asset to Amazon trading post-dotcom-crash in 2001 and projects ETH to ~$40K by 2030, predicated on a doubling of the ETH/BTC ratio.
insights.speaker: Ryan Sean Adams (citing Standard Chartered)
insights.so_what: An institutional sell-side bull case ($40K by 2030 via ratio doubling) gives a long-horizon target; the ETH/BTC ratio is the explicit lever to track for this thesis.
insights.quote: Uh Standard Charter is saying that Ethereum right now ETH the asset is like uh trading like Amazon during 2001... you get ETH by 2030, 40K.

insights.claim: Near's confidential transactions/intents (TEE-based, not ZK cryptography) have reached production adoption—Venice uses it, it enables private ETH-to-ETH transfers routed through Near, and works as cross-chain privacy infrastructure for any integrator—driving real Near revenue and Near's price strength.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: Adoption-plus-revenue, not just narrative, is what separated Near in this rotation; usage-backed privacy infra is the bull signal, and Near is dominating the intents space despite earlier movers.
insights.quote: this is one of the reasons why Near is up so much because this has actually seen adoption and has led to a bunch of Near revenue.

insights.claim: Block's Cash App (60M MAUs) added multi-chain USDC support (Ethereum, Polygon, Arbitrum, Solana), and Near private intents can route Cash App funds privately to other chains such as Zcash.
insights.speaker: Ryan Sean Adams / David Hoffman
insights.so_what: Mainstream fintech distribution (60M users) plus privacy-routing rails expands real on-ramp demand for stablecoins and privacy assets like Zcash; watch consumer-app integrations as a demand vector.
insights.quote: you can go from cash app to near private intents to any other blockchain. Zcash for example

insights.claim: Privacy is the recurring thread tying the winners together: the alts pumping (Zcash, Railgun, Near/Venice via confidential intents) and the EF's refocus all center on privacy, suggesting privacy is the use case the market is rewarding in this regime.
insights.speaker: host synthesis
insights.so_what: Privacy is emerging as the dominant surviving narrative; a thematic basket of privacy infra/assets is where capital and roadmap focus are concentrating.

insights.claim: The founder of OpenZeppelin has been privately advising friends and family to exit ALL DeFi positions—including low-risk blue chips like MakerDAO and Compound—citing AI coding agents now superhuman at finding smart-contract vulnerabilities.
insights.speaker: Ryan Sean Adams (citing OpenZeppelin founder)
insights.so_what: A premier smart-contract security firm's founder calling for a full DeFi exit on AI-exploit risk is a tail-risk flag specific to Ethereum DeFi; reassess protocol exposure under an AI-attacker threat model.
insights.quote: I've been privately advising friends and family to exit all DeFi positions, including low-risk blue chips like a maker dowo and compound due to coding agents that are superhuman at finding vulnerabilities.

insights.claim: Leading DeFi founders pushed back hard with data: Stani (Aave) called DeFi infra materially more resilient than prior cycles; Mark Zeller said <10% of past-year DeFi issues were code bugs; Jack Sanford said of ~$630M lost in April 2026, over 96% was unrelated to code security; Hayden Adams (Uniswap) called it a supply-chain/opsec problem, not a DeFi problem.
insights.speaker: Ryan Sean Adams (citing Stani, Zeller, Sanford, Hayden Adams)
insights.so_what: The counter-data reframes losses as opsec/supply-chain rather than contract-code failures; the real risk vector to harden is keys/dependencies, not necessarily audited contract logic.
insights.quote: $630 million was lo lost in April 2026. Unfortunately, over 96% of those losses were unrelated to code security.

themes.theme: Macro backdrop: Iran, oil, yields, AI equities

insights.claim: An Iran peace deal is reportedly near signing (leaked terms: reopen Strait of Hormuz, US lifts blockade, 60-day ceasefire, Hormuz traffic restored within 30 days), but it defers the nuclear concessions to a separate 60-day window, making it a peace deal rather than a nuclear deal, alongside a simultaneous re-escalation of daily kinetic strikes.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: Markets priced the leaked deal as legitimate (S&P ATHs, oil down), but unresolved nuclear terms plus ongoing strikes keep tail risk live; a signing or breakdown is a near-term oil/risk catalyst.
insights.quote: this is firmly a peace deal and not a nuclear concessions deal because Iran really does not want to concede nuclear concessions because it's part of their identity

insights.claim: Brent crude fell back to 'wartime lows' of ~$93-94 (vs prior wartime highs ~$112), but David argues markets need oil to break sustainably below ~$90 to really feel good.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: A clean break below $90 oil is the host's threshold for a risk-on macro green light; watch that level as a gating condition for crypto/risk recovery.
insights.quote: we really need oil to break down below a like $90 and meaningfully and move there.

insights.claim: 10-year Treasury yields rose even as oil fell this week, breaking their usual co-movement, which David reads as a stagflation warning: sustained high oil feeds into inflation and yields regardless of an Iran resolution.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: Yields decoupling upward from falling oil is an unfavorable, stagflationary signal; even a peace deal may not relieve the rate/inflation pressure weighing on risk assets.
insights.quote: Yields, 10-year yields are up despite oil being down. And that is not good... The stagflation word comes to mind.

insights.claim: Micron surged into a ~$1.066 trillion market cap, turning $1,000 a year ago into ~$100,000 (a ~100x), illustrating that the AI/memory equity trade is producing crypto-2021-style parabolic momentum charts.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman / Ryan Sean Adams
insights.so_what: Capital is finding crypto-like asymmetric returns inside AI/memory equities instead of crypto; the relative pull of the AI trade is a direct headwind for crypto flows.
insights.quote: If you had $1,000 of micro stock, Micron stock a year ago, you would now have $100,000.

insights.claim: David frames the AI rally as a 'rolling sector' rotation (memory, then others) analogous to crypto's sector-rotation 'roller coaster,' and argues the AI 'hot ball of money' is currently snowballing/getting bigger rather than leaking and falling apart.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: As long as the AI money snowballs and rotates between sub-sectors, it keeps drawing capital away from crypto; a stall in that snowball would be the signal capital might rotate back.
insights.quote: the question is like when it moves from sector to sector does the hot ball of money like snowball or does it leak and fall apart? And right now I think you can definitely argue that it is snowballing

insights.claim: Hosts flag a '2021-like' speculative top tell: with equities at ATHs, alternative collectibles like Pokemon cards (and tokenized Pokemon card marketplaces), baseball cards and Rolexes are also pricing at all-time highs.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: Broad-based collectible euphoria is a classic late-cycle/froth signal; a warning sign that risk appetite may be peaking even as crypto majors weaken.
insights.quote: it feels a little 2021y in that like equity markets are at alltime highs and then like weird [ __ ] like baseball cards and Rolexes are being priced at all time high.

insights.claim: SoFi launched SoFi USD, claiming the first US national-bank-issued, GENIUS-Act-compliant stablecoin embedded directly into a retail banking app (~15M users); on-chain it shows ~$100M on Ethereum versus only ~$26,000 on Solana.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman / Ryan Sean Adams
insights.so_what: Bank-issued, compliant stablecoins distributed to retail are arriving, and Ethereum is capturing the overwhelming majority of issuance vs Solana—an ETH settlement-layer tailwind even amid ETH price weakness.
insights.quote: If you go to the SoFi stablecoin on Salana, there's 26,000 uh dollars worth of SoFi. If you go to Ethereum, there's a hundred million.

insights.claim: Andrew Kang (back from a robotics detour) launched 'Robo Strategy' (NASDAQ: BOT), a closed-end fund holding private robotics/physical-AI names (Figure AI, Apptronik, Dyna Robotics, Standard Bots) using a MicroStrategy-style ATM—issuing equity to buy more when shares trade above NAV—with Nick Carter joining as independent director.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: Crypto operators are porting the MicroStrategy treasury playbook into AI/robotics equities; the model only works while shares trade above NAV and buyers pay the premium—two big ifs to watch.
insights.quote: if the NAV trades above if if the asset trades above NAV, they will issue new equity to purchase more shares of the robotics company. So, it's kind of doing the micro strategy ATM thing.

insights.claim: A pseudonymous claimant ('Noah Do') filed in NY Supreme Court to claim ~3.8M dormant (6-10yr) Satoshi-era Bitcoin (~18% of supply, ~$285B) under 1958-era Finders Keepers/abandoned-property law, having found the wallets via a block explorer and dusted them with notice.
insights.speaker: Ryan Sean Adams / David Hoffman
insights.so_what: A long-shot legal bet (<0.01% odds per hosts) that only pays off in a quantum-recovery scenario where a US entity seizes Satoshi coins; mainly a reminder of the ~18%-of-supply dormant overhang and quantum tail risk.
insights.quote: the only way that this actually pays off for the individual assuming Satoshi's wallets are burned is that quantum comes
